topic: Someone receives a package intended for the previous tenant
raw_output:
[story 1]

The doorbell rang, a sharp, unwelcome intrusion in the late afternoon quiet. I peered through the peephole, my breath catching in my throat. It was a package, taped shut with thick, industrial-grade brown paper, sitting squarely on the welcome mat. A jolt of ice shot through my core. I hadn't even heard from the previous tenant in months; they’d moved out without a word. My hand trembled as I unlatched the door. The air that greeted me smelled faintly of dust and something metallic, like old pennies. When I lifted the box, the weight felt wrong, too dense. A low, involuntary sound escaped my chest. I backed away from the door, my eyes darting to the darkened hallway, every muscle coiled tight, waiting for something to move.

[story 2]

It arrived by courier, a heavy, unmarked crate addressed to the former occupant of Apartment 3B. When the delivery person handed over the slip, my stomach plummeted. This wasn't just mail; this felt like an intrusion, a deliberate trespass into my space. I stood frozen in the hallway, clutching the cardboard like a shield. Every shadow seemed to deepen, stretching and twisting in the slivers of light escaping under the door. My heart hammered against my ribs, a frantic drumbeat I could feel vibrating through my sternum. I couldn't bring myself to look at the box, yet I couldn't leave it there. A bead of sweat traced a cold path down my temple. I took a shaky step back, my gaze locked on the door, convinced that whatever was inside was about to find its way out.

[story 3]

I found the package tucked behind the radiator. It was addressed to the person who lived here before me. As I lifted the wrapping, the sound of my own shallow breathing seemed deafening in the small living room. The paper felt unnervingly cold beneath my fingertips. My vision narrowed, focusing only on the stark, unfamiliar lettering on the exterior. A sudden, sharp wave of nausea washed over me, making my hands shake uncontrollably. I dropped the box onto the worn rug, the thud echoing unnaturally loud. I didn't move to pick it up again. I just stood there, rigid, listening intently to the silence of the apartment, every nerve ending screaming a silent alarm.
